note: The declarative spec of what good looks like. This file is INTENT, not implementation: it describes OUTCOMES a modern web experience should achieve and offers a `detectableVia` HINT for each check, plus Modern Web Guidance (mwg) `guides` and non-mwg `references` the model should consult to set the bar. The hint may mention candidate evidence modalities or tools (Lighthouse, axe, heap snapshot, layout metrics, screenshot, transition video, ad-hoc probe) but it MANDATES NOTHING. The `guides` entries are DECLARATIVE POINTERS into the modern-web-guidance catalog (intent), NOT hard-coded tests: the model consults them up front to learn the recommended approach before judging, then decides which evidence to gather and which tools to run. The `references` entries cover useful non-mwg standards, methods, and optional tooling (for example memory methodology). Nothing here is wired to a code path; there are no hard-coded checks in the runtime. This taxonomy is curated by web-uplift from the sources listed in `sources`; those sources are inputs used to formulate the principles, not authorship of this file. The first five principles adapt Una Kravets' modern-UX framing from her Google I/O 2026 talk 'What's new in Web UI'. The remaining twelve extend the set so a single audit judges modern UX, core task success, the classic Lighthouse quality bars, and the web-quality dimensions the mwg catalog and external frameworks (WCAG/POUR, Nielsen, Inclusive Design, WSG, Resilient Web Design, security/privacy, i18n, anti-dark-pattern) treat as first-class. The expansion and the full coverage map of all 137 mwg guides to principles is documented and justified in docs/principles-analysis.md (adopted 2026-06-13).

sources.title: What's new in Web UI (Google I/O 2026)
sources.speaker: Una Kravets
sources.usedFor: Initial modern-UX principle names and framing for the first five principles.

sources.title: Modern Web Guidance
sources.usedFor: Guide mappings and implementation guidance across the principle checks.

sources.title: Lighthouse
sources.usedFor: Performance, accessibility, best-practices, SEO, and diagnostic quality surfaces.

sources.title: Google RAIL
sources.usedFor: User-centric performance framing for response, animation, idle work, and load.

sources.title: Core Web Vitals
sources.usedFor: LCP, INP, and CLS thresholds for fast and stable experience checks.

sources.title: Baseline
sources.usedFor: Browser-support framing for modern feature adoption and fallbacks.

sources.title: WCAG / POUR
sources.usedFor: Accessibility and inclusive-experience baseline.

sources.title: Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics
sources.usedFor: Trustworthy, predictable, recoverable interaction checks.

sources.title: Inclusive Design Principles
sources.usedFor: Inclusive and comparable-experience framing beyond automated accessibility.

sources.title: W3C Web Sustainability Guidelines
sources.usedFor: Resource efficiency and sustainability checks.

sources.title: Resilient Web Design
sources.usedFor: Progressive enhancement and resilience framing.

sources.title: Progressive enhancement
sources.usedFor: Graceful degradation and no-JavaScript resilience checks.

sources.title: Web security and privacy
sources.usedFor: Security, privacy, data-minimisation, and third-party checks.

sources.title: Progressive Web Apps
sources.usedFor: Installability, service worker, and offline fallback checks for app-like sites.

sources.title: W3C Internationalisation techniques
sources.usedFor: Language, direction, locale, and internationalisation checks.

sources.title: Intl
sources.usedFor: Locale-aware dates, numbers, currencies, durations, calendars, and time-zone handling.

sources.title: Deceptive Design
sources.usedFor: Dark-pattern taxonomy for trustworthy interaction checks.

sources.title: Bringing dark patterns to light
sources.usedFor: Consumer-protection framing for deceptive design and consent/continuity checks.

sources.title: Memory-tracer leak-audit methodology
sources.usedFor: Heap growth, detached DOM, listener accumulation, and bounded-footprint checks.

sources.title: Chrome DevTools MCP skills
sources.usedFor: Optional agent-side debugging workflows for browser evidence, including accessibility, LCP, DevTools usage, troubleshooting, and memory leaks.

sources.title: Chrome DevTools MCP memory-leak-debugging skill
sources.usedFor: Optional memory-leak workflow for heap snapshots, baseline/target/final comparison, memlab analysis, and common leak-pattern diagnosis when an agent has Chrome DevTools MCP available.

applicabilityModel.note: Each principle declares an `applicability` block: `expectation` is either 'default' (expected of essentially every site; absence is a finding) or 'contextual' (legitimately may not apply to some sites; absence is judged, not penalised). `criteria` describes when the principle applies; `optOutWhy` (contextual only) records why a developer might reasonably opt out. The audit may be told a developer's declared scope/opt-outs via web-uplift.json (see schema/config.schema.json): a declared opt-out is reported as `opted-out` (not an issue); for a contextual principle with no declared intent the model makes a judgement call and marks it `not-applicable` WITH a rationale rather than penalising. This is the 'quality without shaming' behaviour.

principles.title: Respect user preferences

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site that renders UI: read the platform's stated and system-level preference signals and adapt.

principles.description: The most fundamental principle for web usability: build trust and improve accessibility by adapting to the user's stated and system-level preferences (theme, motion, contrast) rather than imposing one fixed experience. Read the signals the platform already provides and adjust.

checks.summary: Honours prefers-color-scheme: a usable dark mode exists and is driven by the user's preference (color-scheme / prefers-color-scheme / light-dark()), not hard-coded light only.
checks.detectableVia: HINT (not mandatory): a screenshot or computed background under an emulated prefers-color-scheme: dark condition will reveal whether surfaces re-tint; the page CSS / a color-scheme declaration is corroborating evidence. The model chooses the method.

checks.summary: Honours prefers-reduced-motion: non-essential animations and auto-advance are reduced or removed when the user asks for less motion.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a transition video, or an in-page probe of getAnimations()/computed animation under an emulated prefers-reduced-motion: reduce condition, can show whether motion stops. The model chooses.
guides: respect reduced motion preference accessible animation

checks.summary: Honours prefers-contrast / forced-colors: controls, text and scrollbars remain visible under high-contrast preferences.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a screenshot under emulated prefers-contrast: more / forced-colors, or an axe/contrast probe, can show whether controls and text survive. The model chooses.

principles.title: Implement natural interactions

applicability.criteria: Applies to every interactive site: state, route and gesture changes should feel physical, not abrupt.

principles.description: Make interactions feel intuitive and physical. Use modern declarative motion and transition APIs so movement reflects how things move in the real world, rather than abrupt state swaps or heavy hand-rolled JavaScript.

checks.summary: State and route changes use View Transitions (including same-document, cross-document and scroll-driven/staggered) rather than instant, jarring swaps.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a transition video of a route/state change shows whether it animates; the page source / ::view-transition usage corroborates. The model chooses.
guides: view transitions smooth state route change

checks.summary: Scroll-linked motion (parallax, scrollytelling, entry/exit reveals) uses declarative CSS scroll-driven animations (off main thread) instead of scroll event listeners.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: source/CSS inspection for animation-timeline: scroll()/view(); a long-task / scroll-handler probe can flag the main-thread anti-pattern. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Gesture-driven interactions and entry/exit motion feel native (declarative overscroll/scroll-snap, physics-based easing, animating to intrinsic sizes, pull/swipe gestures) rather than fighting the platform with custom pointer handlers.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: CSS inspection for scroll-snap / overscroll-behavior / physics-based easing vs custom pointermove listeners. The model chooses.
guides: natural gesture interaction scroll snap overscroll

principles.title: Provide guided navigation

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site with more than a single static view: help users understand where they are and guide attention.

principles.description: Help users understand where they are and guide their attention through the experience, using state-aware UI and positioning rather than leaving them to orient themselves.

checks.summary: Sticky/affixed UI reacts to scroll state and position (e.g. the new scroll-state(scrolled) query, shrinking headers, progress indicators) so chrome responds to position instead of static or JS-driven toggling.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a transition video of scrolling, or CSS inspection for scroll-state container queries. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Tooltips, popovers and menus use CSS anchor positioning (with fallback positions) so they stay attached and reposition correctly rather than being manually positioned.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: CSS inspection for anchor-name / position-anchor / position-try on overlays; a screenshot of an open overlay near a viewport edge can show drift. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Navigation and in-page jumps guide attention (highlight effects, scroll/carousel markers, directional transitions, drill-down and drawer navigation) so the user can follow where focus moved.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: CSS inspection for ::highlight / scroll-marker; a transition video can show whether attention is cued after navigation. The model chooses.
guides: scroll marker highlight guide attention navigation

principles.title: Maximize content, reduce noise

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site: prioritise the user's content and use the right semantic, dismissible primitives for any overlays.

principles.description: Prioritise the user's content by eliminating visual clutter, application borders, and intrusive interruptions. Avoid pop-ups and banners that obscure content; use the right semantic, dismissible primitives instead of heavy custom chrome.

checks.summary: No intrusive pop-ups, interstitials or banners that obscure content on load; overlays are dismissible and content-first.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a screenshot on load, or a DOM probe for full-viewport overlays present before interaction. The model chooses.
guides: avoid intrusive interstitial popup content first

checks.summary: Overlays and rich controls use the right primitive: popover (with declarative light-dismiss) for transient UI, dialog for modal flows, details for disclosure, native-but-branded selects and pickers, rather than ad-hoc divs.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: DOM/source inspection for popover / <dialog> / <details> vs custom overlay divs with manual dismiss handling. The model chooses.
guides: popover dialog light dismiss semantic overlay

checks.summary: Minimise non-content chrome and borders so the content is the focus, not the application frame; expressive/decorative visuals serve the content rather than crowd it.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a screenshot plus layout metrics can show the proportion of the viewport given to chrome vs content. The model chooses.

principles.title: Adapt to the form factor

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site: users reach it from a range of viewports and input types, and the layout should genuinely adapt.

principles.description: Account for the form factor users actually access the site from. We design and code on desktop then resize to check small screens, but a modern experience genuinely adapts layout and interaction across viewports and input types using modern responsive techniques.

checks.summary: Layout adapts to narrow viewports with no horizontal overflow and no fixed pixel widths forcing a desktop layout on mobile; viewport meta present; fluid scaling and intrinsic sizing rather than brittle breakpoints.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: layout metrics (scrollWidth vs innerWidth) and a screenshot at an emulated narrow mobile viewport reveal overflow. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Components adapt to their container with container queries (incl. anchored container queries) and content/state-based styling where reused at different sizes, not only global viewport breakpoints.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: CSS inspection for @container / container-type; a computed-style probe of the same component in a wide vs narrow container shows whether it adapts. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Touch targets are adequately sized and hover-only affordances have a non-hover fallback, and keyboard focus is visible, so the UI works for touch, pointer and keyboard alike.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a focus probe (focus an element, read the computed outline) or an axe target-size check; a screenshot of a focused control corroborates. The model chooses.
guides: touch target size hover fallback pointer input focus-visible

principles.title: Support core task success

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site: users should understand the purpose of the page, find the next useful action, complete the primary task, and recover when the flow cannot proceed.

principles.description: The experience should help people accomplish the thing they came to do. A page can use modern APIs, pass accessibility checks and still fail if the purpose is unclear, primary actions are buried, loading or empty states are confusing, or the key journey dead-ends. This principle makes task success explicit: understand the offer, find the path, complete the flow, see system state, and recover.

checks.summary: The page communicates what it is for and exposes the primary next action without requiring users to hunt through decorative content, generic copy, or competing calls to action.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: screenshot the first viewport and key scrolled states; inspect heading structure, nav labels, button text, and visual hierarchy; a task walkthrough can show whether the next action is obvious. The model chooses.
guides: direct user attention primary action visual hierarchy task success

checks.summary: The representative primary flow can be completed end-to-end with predictable steps, no avoidable dead ends, no hidden required information, and no needless detours through modals, account walls, or upsells.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: run the flow manually with screenshots/DOM snapshots at each step; compare expected vs actual path length; inspect form requirements, navigation continuity, and blockers. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Loading, empty, success, error, offline, and partial-completion states are visible and actionable; users can retry, undo, cancel, go back, or continue without losing context.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: exercise network delay/failure, invalid input, empty data and success states; screenshot the state messaging and recovery controls; inspect whether browser history and focus remain sensible. The model chooses.
guides: loading empty error success retry undo cancel state

principles.title: Be fast and stable

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site: load quickly and stay visually stable; good Core Web Vitals are a baseline expectation.

principles.description: Lighthouse 'performance' dimension, expressed as a principle. The page should load quickly and stay visually stable: good Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS), a light main thread, and no layout jank from late-arriving content. Speed and stability are part of modern UX, not separate from it.

checks.summary: Core Web Vitals are in the good range: LCP is fast, interaction latency (INP) is low, and CLS is minimal; work is prioritised and deferred sensibly.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: Lighthouse reports LCP/CLS/TBT directly and the model may run it; layout metrics + a layout-shift observer + a long-task observer (the evidence primitives) give the same signal first-party. The model chooses.
guides: improve core web vitals LCP INP CLS

checks.summary: No cumulative layout shift from late content (images, banners, fonts) that arrives without reserved space.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: the layout primitive's CLS observer captures shifts; a transition video of the first seconds shows content jumping. The model chooses.
guides: prevent layout shift reserve space CLS aspect-ratio

checks.summary: The main thread is not blocked by long tasks or heavy scripting; work is deferred, chunked, prioritised, or moved off-thread.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: the layout primitive records long tasks; a heap summary shows the object population; Lighthouse reports TBT. The model chooses.
guides: reduce main thread work long tasks total blocking time

checks.summary: Critical resources are delivered efficiently: cache headers are useful, render-blocking chains are short, fonts do not block or shift text, HTTP delivery is modern, and network dependency trees do not delay the main content.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a HAR summary can reveal cache headers, redirects, render-blocking candidates, weight offenders and dependency shape; a trace/Lighthouse insight report can corroborate LCP discovery, render-blocking, font-display and document latency. The model chooses.
guides: cache headers render blocking font display modern http network dependency tree

checks.summary: The page does not ship large unused or duplicated JavaScript/CSS, avoidable legacy bundles, or third-party code that dominates the load without user value.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: Lighthouse/trace/code-coverage style evidence can flag unused JS/CSS, duplicated JavaScript and legacy code; a HAR summary shows third-party byte cost and request count. The model chooses.
guides: unused javascript css duplicated javascript third party cost

principles.title: Be inclusive

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site. WCAG conformance is the floor; the Inclusive Design Principles (comparable experience, choice, considering the user's situation) are the ceiling.

principles.description: Widened from the former 'be-accessible' (Lighthouse 'accessibility' dimension). The page is usable with assistive technology and a keyboard AND offers a comparable, inclusive experience beyond bare conformance: sufficient colour contrast, names and roles on controls, labelled form fields, a sensible heading/landmark structure, a visible focus order, legible and well-aligned text, state preserved across DOM moves, and canvas/expressive content exposed to assistive tech. WCAG is the floor; the Inclusive Design Principles (comparable experience, give control, offer choice, consider situation) are the ceiling.

checks.summary: Interactive elements have accessible names, correct roles, and form fields have labels; images have alt text where meaningful; canvas/expressive content is exposed to assistive technology.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: axe-core (injectable via the evaluate primitive) or Lighthouse's a11y audits enumerate these; a DOM probe of the accessibility-relevant attributes is a first-party alternative. The model chooses.
guides: accessible name label role form field aria

checks.summary: Text and essential UI meet WCAG colour-contrast minimums against their background.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: axe contrast rules, a Lighthouse contrast audit, or an in-page probe computing contrast ratios from computed colours. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Heading and landmark structure is logical, focus order follows reading order, keyboard focus is always visible, and interactive state survives DOM moves.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: axe/Lighthouse structural audits; a focus-walk probe (tab through, read activeElement + computed outline) is a first-party alternative. The model chooses.
guides: heading structure landmarks focus order keyboard

checks.summary: Text is legible and inclusively rendered: comfortable line layout, precise alignment, stable rendering across mixed fonts, no clipping or cramped wrapping that harms comprehension.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a screenshot of body and heading text, plus CSS inspection for text-wrap / text alignment / font fallback handling. The model chooses.

checks.summary: The experience remains usable when zoomed or reflowed, touch targets are large enough, media has captions or equivalents where needed, and the viewport does not prevent user scaling.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: Lighthouse/axe target-size, meta-viewport and media-caption audits are useful signals; screenshots at narrow and zoomed conditions plus DOM/media inspection can corroborate. The model chooses.
guides: target size zoom reflow captions viewport user scalable

principles.title: Follow best practices

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site: it should be soundly built and served. Security now lives in be-private-and-secure and forms/humane handling in be-trustworthy; this principle is no longer the catch-all for those.

principles.description: Lighthouse 'best practices' dimension, NARROWED so it is not a catch-all. It means the page is soundly BUILT and SERVED: no console errors or uncaught exceptions, a valid doctype and charset, correctly sized images, no deprecated APIs, and well-structured, non-repetitive CSS and HTML. It explicitly HANDS OFF security and data handling to be-private-and-secure, and forms / humane error handling to be-trustworthy. Do not raise security or forms findings here; cite the dedicated principle instead.

checks.summary: The page loads without console errors or uncaught exceptions.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: capture Runtime/Log CDP events, or a probe that reads collected errors; Lighthouse reports this too. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Valid doctype and charset, images sized with correct aspect ratio, no deprecated APIs misused, and CSS/HTML are well structured and not needlessly repetitive. (HTTPS, CSP and permission hygiene are judged under be-private-and-secure, not here.)
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a DOM/source probe for doctype/charset/img dimensions; CSS inspection for repetition; Lighthouse best-practices audits cover the rest. The model chooses.
guides: valid doctype charset image aspect ratio deprecated api best practices

checks.summary: The page uses the platform cleanly: no deprecated APIs, no avoidable BFCache blockers, no broken source maps or inspector issues, no stale vulnerable libraries, no paste-prevention on inputs, and no notification/geolocation prompts on load.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: Lighthouse best-practices audits and DevTools inspector/deprecation signals can surface these; DOM/source probes can verify paste handlers and prompt timing. The model chooses.
guides: baseline deprecated api bfcache source maps inspector issues paste prevention geolocation notification on load

principles.title: Be discoverable

applicability.criteria: Applies to essentially every public site. A site deliberately gated behind auth or noindex (an internal tool, a private app) may legitimately opt out of public discoverability; judge accordingly.

principles.description: Lighthouse 'SEO' dimension, expressed as a principle. The page is discoverable and shareable: a descriptive title and meta description, crawlable links, a valid robots configuration, a viewport meta tag, and meaningful link text. The Modern Web Guidance catalog has no SEO-specific guide, so this principle is judged from the page itself (title, meta, crawlable links) rather than from mwg.

checks.summary: The page has a unique, descriptive <title> and a meta description.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a DOM probe reads <title> and meta[name=description]; Lighthouse SEO audits cover the same ground. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Links are crawlable (real href), there is a viewport meta tag, robots does not block indexing, and link text is descriptive.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a DOM probe for anchor hrefs, viewport meta, and robots; Lighthouse SEO audits corroborate. The model chooses.
guides: crawlable links viewport meta robots descriptive link text

checks.summary: Public pages expose the indexing signals search engines need: successful HTTP status, canonical URL when appropriate, hreflang for localized variants, robots/sitemap consistency, and no accidental noindex/noarchive policy.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: inspect response status and headers, <link rel=canonical>, hreflang links, robots meta, robots.txt and sitemap.xml; Lighthouse SEO audits cover several of these. The model chooses.
guides: canonical hreflang http status robots sitemap noindex seo indexing

checks.summary: Where the page represents an article, product, organization, event, place or other rich entity, structured data and share-preview metadata accurately describe the visible content.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: inspect JSON-LD/microdata and social preview tags against visible content; Lighthouse has a manual structured-data audit, and ad-hoc probes can parse schema.org blocks. The model chooses.
guides: structured data schema.org json-ld open graph social preview metadata

principles.title: Be private and secure

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site. Protecting users by default (HTTPS, sensible CSP, secure cookies, in-context permissions, data minimisation, modern auth) is a baseline expectation, not optional.

principles.description: The site protects users by default. Served over HTTPS with a sensible Content-Security-Policy, secure/SameSite cookies, no over-collection or leaky third parties, permission prompts requested in context (not on load), data-minimised analytics, and modern auth (passkeys over passwords where relevant). This is the single biggest gap the former follow-best-practices left open; it now owns security and data handling.

checks.summary: Served over HTTPS with a sensible Content-Security-Policy and secure, SameSite cookies; no mixed content or obvious transport/header smells.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: inspect response headers / page protocol via an evaluate probe or the network layer; Lighthouse best-practices flags HTTPS and CSP issues. The model chooses.
guides: https csp secure cookies content security policy

checks.summary: No over-collection of user data, no leaky third parties, and analytics/background data collection is minimised and batched rather than chatty or session-wide by default.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: inspect network requests and third-party origins; a probe of analytics/beacon calls. The model chooses.
guides: data minimisation third party privacy analytics

checks.summary: Permission prompts are requested in context (not on load), and authentication uses modern, phishing-resistant methods (passkeys / WebAuthn) where relevant rather than passwords alone.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a probe for permission requests fired on load; source inspection for passkey / WebAuthn / navigator.credentials usage in auth flows. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Browser-enforced defenses are configured where appropriate: HSTS, clickjacking protection (frame-ancestors / X-Frame-Options), Trusted Types for XSS-sensitive apps, origin isolation, privacy-preserving third-party cookie posture, and sensible Referrer-Policy / Permissions-Policy.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: inspect response headers and browser security state; Lighthouse/DevTools security audits can corroborate HSTS, clickjacking, Trusted Types, origin isolation and third-party cookie findings. The model chooses.
guides: hsts clickjacking frame-ancestors trusted types origin isolation referrer policy permissions policy third-party cookies

principles.title: Be resilient

applicability.criteria: Applies wherever core content or primary flows must survive adverse conditions (no JS, slow/offline network, older or non-Baseline browsers). Most content and commerce sites qualify.
applicability.optOutWhy: A developer may reasonably opt out of the offline/installable aspects for a tool that is intrinsically online-only (e.g. a live collaborative editor, a real-time trading dashboard) or an internal app on a controlled, modern-browser fleet. Progressive enhancement of core content is still expected even then; the offline/PWA portion is what is contextual.

principles.description: Core content and primary flows work under adverse conditions: no JS, slow or offline network, older or non-Baseline browsers. Modern features are progressive enhancements with graceful fallbacks; where the site is an app, it is installable and offers an offline fallback. This makes the fix-mode Baseline rule (use modern features, but degrade gracefully below Baseline Widely available) an explicit principle.

checks.summary: Core content and primary flows are reachable and usable without JavaScript and on older or non-Baseline browsers; modern features layer on as enhancements with fallbacks, and reactive/transition state stabilises rather than flickering before it settles.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: load with scripting disabled or compare a no-JS fetch of the HTML against the rendered page; check for Baseline-aware fallbacks in source. The model chooses.
guides: progressive enhancement graceful degradation no javascript baseline fallback

checks.summary: The page behaves robustly at runtime: overlays and menus never get cut off, DOM state survives moves, background work and async dependencies are sequenced and conditional rather than fragile, and initial visibility state is detected correctly.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: exercise menus near viewport edges with a screenshot; a probe of async/visibility behaviour. The model chooses.

checks.summary: Where the site is an app, it is installable (web app manifest) and offers an offline fallback and works on flaky networks. (Contextual: a brochure or intrinsically-online site may reasonably not need this.)
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a probe for a service worker registration and a web app manifest; test behaviour offline. The model chooses.
guides: offline fallback service worker web app manifest installable pwa

checks.summary: HTTP errors, network failures, timeouts and stale data states are handled intentionally: users see useful recovery options rather than blank screens, infinite spinners, broken shells, or misleading success states.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: simulate failed fetches/offline mode or inspect representative 404/500 routes; screenshots and DOM snapshots of error/loading/empty states show whether recovery is possible. The model chooses.
guides: http error network failure timeout retry stale data resilient state

principles.title: Be internationalised

applicability.criteria: Applies to any site that serves, or could plausibly serve, users in more than one language, locale or time zone, or that handles dates, numbers, currencies or recurring events.
applicability.optOutWhy: A developer may reasonably opt out for a deliberately single-locale, single-language site with no locale-sensitive data (e.g. a local-only service in one region). Even then, correct lang/dir and logical properties are cheap; the locale-aware data and translation portions are what is contextual.

principles.description: The site works for users in any language and locale. Correct lang/dir, logical CSS properties, locale-aware dates, numbers, currencies and calendar systems, translation-ready markup (and on-device translation/language detection where useful), and time handling that survives time zones and DST.

checks.summary: Correct lang and dir attributes, logical CSS properties (inline/block) rather than physical left/right, and translation-ready markup so the layout and reading order survive other languages and writing modes.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a DOM probe for <html lang>/dir and CSS inspection for logical vs physical properties. The model chooses.
guides: lang dir logical properties translation ready bidi

checks.summary: Dates, numbers, currencies, durations and calendar systems are formatted locale-aware (Intl), location-agnostic where stored, and recurring intervals and event differentials are modelled correctly.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: source inspection for Intl.* usage vs hand-rolled formatting; a probe of rendered dates/numbers under a different locale. The model chooses.
guides: locale aware dates numbers currency intl calendar

checks.summary: Time handling survives time zones and DST: events coordinate across zones, partial time concepts are modelled, and stored times are unambiguous.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: source inspection for time-zone-aware date handling vs naive local Date math. The model chooses.
guides: time zone dst event coordination partial time

principles.title: Be trustworthy

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site: be honest and humane. No dark patterns, honest defaults, easy reversal, and humane error handling that prevents and recovers from mistakes.

principles.description: The site is honest and humane. No dark patterns (confirmshaming, forced continuity, disguised ads, nagging consent walls), honest defaults, clear pricing and consent, easy reversal of actions, predictable controls, and humane error handling that prevents and recovers from mistakes rather than blaming the user. Covers the Nielsen heuristics and WCAG-Understandable criteria (error prevention, recovery, user control, consistency) and the humane-forms guides; distinct from maximize-content-reduce-noise, which bans the FORM of interruption but not the deceptive INTENT.

checks.summary: No deceptive design: no confirmshaming, forced continuity, disguised ads, or nagging consent walls; honest defaults; clear pricing and consent; easy reversal/cancel; predictable, declaratively-wired actions; and no hidden-text tricks (hidden content stays deep-linkable and indexable rather than used to deceive).
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a screenshot of consent/upsell/cancel flows; source inspection for declarative button actions vs misleading controls. The model chooses.
guides: honest defaults no dark patterns confirmshaming forced continuity consent

checks.summary: Forms prevent and recover from mistakes humanely: validate after interaction (not prematurely), give clear required-field feedback, announce errors accessibly, and signal invalid fields visibly rather than blaming the user.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: exercise a form, submit invalid input, and observe timing and clarity of errors via a screenshot or a :user-invalid / aria-invalid probe. The model chooses.
guides: humane form validation error prevention recovery user-invalid

checks.summary: Input is assisted, not obstructed: correct autocomplete tokens so address, payment, sign-in and sign-up fields autofill, and inputs are highlighted/sized to help the user rather than trip them up.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: source/DOM inspection for autocomplete attributes on form fields; a probe of autofill affordances. The model chooses.
guides: autocomplete autofill address payment sign-in input assistance

checks.summary: Checkout, subscription, consent, authentication and account-management flows are clear, reversible, and proportionate: pricing and commitments are visible, cancellation is findable, sensitive actions re-authenticate when appropriate, and users are not tricked into continuity.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: walkthrough checkout/subscription/auth/account flows when present; screenshot pricing, confirmation, cancellation and reauthentication states; inspect passkey/autocomplete support for sign-in and payment. The model chooses.
guides: clear pricing consent cancellation subscription checkout account flow

principles.title: Be sustainable

applicability.criteria: Applies to every site as a lens on resource efficiency, but is judged with proportion: a media-heavy product (video platform, game) legitimately ships more bytes than a text site.
applicability.optOutWhy: A developer is not opting out of efficiency, but the absolute page-weight bar is contextual: a rich media or 3D experience cannot meet a text-site weight budget. Judge whether assets are optimised and work is non-wasteful FOR WHAT THE SITE IS, not against a fixed byte count.

principles.description: The site is resource-efficient. It respects a page-weight budget appropriate to its purpose, ships optimised assets at appropriate resolutions, avoids wasteful background work and over-fetching, and prefers the lightest technique that achieves the result. Overlaps performance on outcomes (lighter is faster) but its intent (resource/carbon efficiency) is distinct, so the auditor weighs asset weight and wasted work, not just perceived speed. Backed by the W3C Web Sustainability Guidelines.

checks.summary: Images and decorative assets are optimised and served at appropriate resolutions; decorative pseudo-element imagery and heavy decorative images are resolution-optimised rather than oversized.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: inspect transferred image bytes vs displayed size; source inspection for modern formats and resolution handling. The model chooses.
guides: optimized images appropriate resolution page weight budget

checks.summary: Background work and fetching are not wasteful: background processing is efficient and de-prioritised, and the lightest technique that achieves the result is preferred over heavy or redundant work.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a long-task / network probe for background fetches and processing while idle or backgrounded. The model chooses.
guides: avoid wasteful background work over-fetching carbon efficiency

checks.summary: Third-party scripts, fonts, video, audio, animation and heavy media are proportionate to the user value they provide; autoplay or background media is avoided unless essential and resource use is cached or deferred where possible.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a HAR summary shows third-party bytes, font/media weight and caching; screenshots/video reveal autoplay and decorative media; trace/layout evidence shows whether media/animation keeps work running. The model chooses.
guides: third-party scripts fonts video autoplay media budget cache resource efficiency

principles.title: Be agent ready

applicability.criteria: Applies to sites that want AI agents and on-device intelligence to use their capabilities safely and in a structured way (e.g. agentic commerce, productivity tools, content with summarisation value).
applicability.optOutWhy: Forward-looking and emerging. Many sites legitimately do not (yet) expose agent-facing capabilities, and there is no settled external framework mandating it. Absence is expected, not a failing; flag opportunities, do not penalise. Opt out for any site with no agent-facing surface.

principles.description: Forward-looking. The site exposes its capabilities to AI agents and on-device intelligence in a structured, safe way (WebMCP tools, agentic forms, agentic JavaScript tools) and uses on-device inference (language model, summariser) appropriately. The only principle that homes the WebMCP / on-device-AI cluster; emerging, so judged as opportunity rather than obligation.

checks.summary: Where it makes sense, the site exposes structured, safe capabilities to agents via WebMCP tools, agentic forms, and agentic JavaScript tools rather than leaving agents to scrape and guess.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: source inspection for WebMCP / agentic-tool registration and agent-readable affordances. The model chooses.
guides: webmcp agentic tools agent ready machine readable

checks.summary: On-device inference (built-in language model, summariser) is used appropriately where it improves the experience, rather than shipping every task to a server.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: source inspection for built-in AI (language model / summariser) usage. The model chooses.
guides: on-device built-in ai language model summarizer

principles.title: Be memory-efficient

applicability.criteria: Memory hygiene applies to every site: a page should not leak memory or grow its footprint without bound. The bounded-footprint and no-detached-dom-or-unbounded-listeners checks are judgeable on any page from a single state. The no-leak-under-repeated-interaction check is CONTEXTUAL on whether the page actually HAS a representative interaction to repeat: it is most meaningful for long-lived and SPA sessions (single-page apps, infinite feeds, editors, dashboards) where listeners, detached DOM, caches, and timers accumulate over a session. For a static one-shot page with no repeated interaction, do not force a synthetic interaction: mark that check not-applicable WITH a rationale (lower-risk, nothing to repeat) rather than penalising the page.
applicability.optOutWhy: The principle as a whole is default (every page should be free of leaks and unbounded growth), but the leak-under-repeated-interaction test depends on there being a real, representative interaction to repeat ~10x. A static page with no such interaction legitimately has nothing to exercise, so that specific check is judged not-applicable with a rationale; the bounded-footprint and detached-DOM/listener checks still apply.

principles.description: The page should not leak memory or grow its footprint without bound, especially under repeated interaction. Long-lived and single-page-app sessions degrade and eventually crash when event listeners, detached DOM nodes, caches, observers, and timers accumulate and are never released. Memory hygiene is part of modern UX: a session that gets slower and heavier the longer it is used is a broken experience even if the first paint was fast. Derived from the memory-tracer leak-audit methodology (baseline heap snapshot, repeat a representative interaction, post snapshot, compare retained growth), with the Chrome DevTools MCP memory-leak-debugging skill listed as an optional corroborating workflow when that server is available.

checks.summary: Repeating a representative interaction (open/close a modal, navigate a route and back, infinite-scroll a list) about 10 times does not grow retained heap without bound; what is allocated during the interaction is released when it ends.
checks.detectableVia: HINT (not mandatory): compare heap snapshots for retained growth - a baseline, then one taken after repeating the interaction with `--interact` about 10x (the memory-tracer methodology: baseline -> repeat -> post -> compare). Performance.getMetrics (JSHeapUsedSize, Nodes) across the same before/after window is corroboration. If Chrome DevTools MCP is available, follow its memory-leak-debugging skill: capture baseline, target, and final snapshots, then use memlab or the provided comparison workflow rather than reading raw .heapsnapshot files directly. The package-native `heap` primitive remains the default path. This check is only meaningful where the page has a real interaction to repeat; for a static page with none, mark it not-applicable with a rationale rather than fabricating one. The model chooses.

references.usedFor: Baseline -> repeated interaction -> post snapshot comparison of retained heap growth.
Optional Chrome DevTools MCP skill workflow: baseline, target and final snapshots, memlab analysis, and leak trace investigation.

checks.summary: Heap size and DOM node count are reasonable for what the page is; the footprint is proportionate rather than bloated.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: a single `heap` summary's totals (nodeCount, totalSelfSizeBytes, constructor population) plus Performance.getMetrics (Nodes, JSHeapUsedSize) give the current footprint to judge against the page's purpose. Chrome DevTools MCP heap snapshots and memlab snapshot analysis can provide the same memory distribution when available. Read summaries or derived analysis, never raw snapshots unless a dedicated heap-analysis tool is doing the analysis. The model chooses.

references.usedFor: Proportionate footprint judgement from heap totals and constructor distribution.
Optional Chrome DevTools MCP skill workflow for heap snapshot capture and memlab-based footprint analysis.

checks.summary: No growing population of detached DOM nodes, and no ever-accumulating event listeners or timers that are added but never removed across a session.
checks.detectableVia: HINT: the `heap` summary by constructor (Detached* nodes) compared across a before/after pair shows a growing detached-DOM population; an `evaluate` probe can sample listener/timer counts (e.g. getEventListeners-style counting, or instrumenting addEventListener/setInterval) before and after the repeated interaction to spot growth. Chrome DevTools MCP heap snapshots plus the memory-leak-debugging skill's common-leak guidance can corroborate detached DOM, listeners, closures, globals, and unbounded caches. Caveat from the memory-tracer and Chrome DevTools MCP guidance: detached nodes can be intentional caches, so judge confidence rather than asserting a bug. The model chooses.

references.usedFor: Before/after detached DOM, listener and timer accumulation checks.
Optional Chrome DevTools MCP skill workflow for retained detached DOM, listener, closure, global and cache leak investigation.
